description: Decision tree configurations for automated migration path selection. Each tree is a directed acyclic graph of condition nodes and leaf (result) nodes. Traversal starts at 'root'.

nodeSchema.description: Shape of each node in a decision tree

conditionNode.question: string — what to evaluate about the BizTalk artifact or intent
conditionNode.evaluates: string — dot-path into MigrationResult or IntegrationIntent to check
conditionNode.yes: string — node ID if condition is true
conditionNode.no: string — node ID if condition is false

result.rationale: string — why this recommendation
result.connectorId: string? — Logic Apps connector/service ID if applicable
result.warnings: string[]? — things to watch out for

skuSelection.description: Determine the correct Logic Apps SKU for a migration scenario

check_onprem.question: Does the application connect to on-premises or private network systems?

check_vnet_acceptable.question: Is VNET injection acceptable (managed Azure compute with private networking)?

check_long_running.question: Do any orchestrations run for more than 90 days or require stateful dehydration?

check_biztalk_migration.question: Is this a BizTalk Server migration (as opposed to net-new development)?

check_simple_stateless.question: Is the workflow simple, stateless, and cloud-only?

result.recommendation: Logic Apps Standard with VNET Integration
result.rationale: On-premises connectivity requires private network access. Standard plan supports VNET injection.
warnings: VNET integration requires Standard plan (Workflow Standard or higher)
Premium hosting plan required for VNET injection
nextSteps: Configure VNET integration in Logic Apps Standard resource
Set up private endpoints for Azure services (Service Bus, Storage)
Install on-premises data gateway for connectors that require it

result.rationale: VNET injection not acceptable. Use Logic Apps Standard with Azure Arc to run on-premises.
warnings: Hybrid mode requires Azure Arc-enabled Kubernetes or Container Apps
nextSteps: Set up Azure Arc-enabled infrastructure on-premises
Deploy Logic Apps Standard Hybrid extension
Configure connected environment

result.rationale: Long-running orchestrations require stateful execution with checkpoint persistence. Standard stateful workflows support up to 1-year run duration.
nextSteps: Use Stateful workflow type (not Stateless)
Configure Azure Storage for checkpoint persistence
Design timer-based escalation using Delay-Until action

result.recommendation: Logic Apps Standard (dedicated plan, tuned concurrency)
result.rationale: High throughput requires dedicated compute and configurable concurrency settings.
nextSteps: Set runtimeConfiguration.concurrency in workflow triggers
Configure host.json extensionBundle and scaling settings
Monitor with Application Insights and set throughput alerts

result.recommendation: Logic Apps Standard (recommended default for all BizTalk migrations)
result.rationale: Standard SKU provides stateful execution matching BizTalk's orchestration model, built-in ServiceProvider connectors for better performance, and VNET integration capability.
nextSteps: Create Logic Apps Standard resource in Azure
Configure Application Insights for monitoring
Set up Key Vault for credential storage

result.recommendation: Logic Apps Consumption (acceptable for simple stateless workflows, but Standard preferred)
result.rationale: Simple stateless cloud-only workflows can use Consumption plan. However, Standard is still preferred for consistency in migration projects.
warnings: Consumption does not support built-in (ServiceProvider) connectors — all connectors are managed

connectorType.description: Determine the correct Logic Apps connector type for a given BizTalk adapter

check_builtin_available.question: Is a built-in (ServiceProvider) connector available for this adapter's protocol?

check_managed_available.question: Is a managed ApiConnection connector available in the Logic Apps connector catalog?

check_needs_gateway.question: Does the target require on-premises or private network connectivity?

check_http_accessible.question: Is the target accessible via HTTP/REST (internet or private with VNET)?

check_dotnet_client.question: Is there a .NET client library available for this proprietary protocol?

result.rationale: Built-in connectors run in-process with the Logic Apps runtime — lower latency, no extra managed connector hop, simpler config in connections.json.
nextSteps: Add serviceProviderConnections entry in connections.json
Add connection string to App Settings (reference Key Vault)
Use serviceProviderConfiguration in action inputs

result.rationale: No built-in connector available but a managed connector exists in the catalog.
nextSteps: Create API Connection resource in Azure
Add managedApiConnections entry in connections.json
Authenticate via OAuth, managed identity, or API key per connector docs

result.recommendation: Use managed connector + On-Premises Data Gateway
result.rationale: Managed connector available but target is on-premises. On-premises data gateway bridges Logic Apps to the private network.
nextSteps: Install On-Premises Data Gateway on a machine with network access to the target
Register gateway in Azure Portal
Associate gateway with the API connection
Verify gateway machine has required drivers (SQL, SAP, etc.)

result.rationale: No specific connector needed. The HTTP action handles any REST/SOAP endpoint with configurable method, headers, and auth.
nextSteps: Configure HTTP action with method, URI, headers, body
Set authentication: Managed Identity, Basic, OAuth, or Certificate
Add retry policy for resilience

result.recommendation: Build Azure Function wrapping the proprietary protocol
result.rationale: .NET client library available but protocol not supported natively in Logic Apps. Azure Functions (.NET) can host the client and expose it as HTTP.
nextSteps: Create Azure Functions (.NET 8 isolated) project
Implement protocol client (WCF/TCP/proprietary) in the function
Expose as HTTP trigger
Call Azure Function from Logic Apps via HTTP action

result.recommendation: Redesign required — no migration path available
result.rationale: No connector, HTTP access, or .NET client available for this protocol. The endpoint must be redesigned to expose an accessible interface.
warnings: This is a blocking gap — migration cannot proceed without endpoint changes
WCF-NetNamedPipe is a common example of this scenario
nextSteps: Work with the system owner to expose an HTTP REST or Service Bus endpoint
OR: Use Azure Relay for WCF scenarios with existing service listeners
OR: Accept this as a deferred item and migrate other workflows first

transformationApproach.description: Determine the correct transformation tool for a BizTalk map (.btm)

check_source_format.question: Is the source data format XML (vs JSON, flat-file, EDI)?

check_edi_format.question: Is the source EDI (X12 or EDIFACT)?

check_flat_file.question: Is the source a flat file (CSV, fixed-width, positional)?

check_scripting_functoids.question: Does the map contain scripting functoids (msxsl:script C# blocks)?

check_script_complexity.question: Are the scripting functoids doing simple string/math operations (no external calls, no complex logic)?

check_map_complexity.question: Does the map have fewer than 20 links and use only basic functoids?

check_target_format.question: Is the target format JSON (vs XML)?

result.rationale: Simple XML→XML mapping with basic functoids. LML is the modern Logic Apps mapping format with designer support.
nextSteps: Open VS Code with Logic Apps extension
Create new Data Map (.lml) file
Import source and target schemas
Recreate links and functoid logic as LML expressions

result.recommendation: Data Mapper LML (native JSON output support)
result.rationale: LML supports JSON output natively, making it the preferred choice for XML→JSON or JSON→JSON transformations.
nextSteps: Define JSON schema for target
Create LML map with JSON target schema
Use LML expression syntax for field mapping

result.rationale: Complex XML→XML mapping. XSLT provides full transformation power. BizTalk generates XSLT from BTM maps — review and clean up the output.
warnings: BizTalk-generated XSLT contains msxsl:script blocks — these MUST be removed
Logic Apps XSLT action uses .NET XslCompiledTransform (XSLT 1.0, no extension objects)
Test generated XSLT thoroughly after cleanup
nextSteps: Extract compiled XSLT from BizTalk .btm map
Remove all msxsl:script blocks
Replace C# extension calls with XSLT 1.0 equivalents
Upload to Logic Apps Standard artifact folder or Integration Account

result.recommendation: XSLT with scripting functoids rewritten as pure XSLT 1.0
result.rationale: Scripting functoids do simple operations that can be expressed in XSLT 1.0 without C# extension objects.
warnings: Remove all msxsl:script and userCSharp namespace declarations
Replace string operations: use concat(), substring(), string-length() etc.
Date calculations: approximate with XSLT string functions or accept UTC-only output
nextSteps: Extract XSLT from .btm
For each msxsl:script block: identify the operation and replace with XSLT equivalent
Test with same input/output fixtures used for BizTalk

result.recommendation: Azure Functions (custom transformation with full C# capabilities)
result.rationale: Complex scripting functoids with external calls, complex date math, or custom .NET logic cannot be expressed in XSLT 1.0 or LML.
nextSteps: Create Azure Functions (.NET 8 isolated) project
Port the scripting functoid C# code to the function
Accept XML input, return transformed XML/JSON
Call from Logic Apps via HTTP action
Unit test the function independently

result.recommendation: Integration Account X12 Decode / EDIFACT Decode action
result.rationale: EDI parsing is handled by Logic Apps Integration Account EDI decode actions — no custom map needed for parsing.
nextSteps: Create Integration Account and link to Logic Apps Standard
Configure trading partners and agreements
Use X12 Decode or EDIFACT Decode action in workflow
Map decoded XML fields using LML or XSLT as needed

result.rationale: No native flat file disassembler in Logic Apps. Azure Functions can parse CSV/fixed-width/positional formats using .NET libraries.
nextSteps: Create Azure Functions project
Implement flat file parser using CsvHelper, TextFieldParser, or custom code
Return parsed data as JSON array
Logic Apps processes the JSON array with ForEach action

messagingService.description: Determine the correct Azure messaging service for a given messaging scenario

check_ordering_required.question: Is ordered message processing required (sequential convoy, FIFO per correlation key)?

check_fanout.question: Does the same message need to be delivered to many independent consumers (fan-out)?

check_many_subscribers.question: Are there more than 10 subscribers or are subscribers dynamically created?

check_replacing_msmq.question: Is this replacing MSMQ or WCF-NetMsmq?

result.recommendation: Azure Service Bus Queue with Sessions
result.rationale: Service Bus sessions provide FIFO ordering within a session (= BizTalk correlation set value). Multiple consumers can process different sessions in parallel.
nextSteps: Create session-enabled Service Bus queue
Producers set SessionId = correlation key when sending
Logic Apps trigger uses receiveMode: peekLock with session support

result.rationale: Event Hubs handles millions of events per second with consumer groups for independent processing. Ideal for replacing high-volume BizTalk Event Hub or custom TCP receivers.
nextSteps: Create Event Hubs namespace + hub
Configure consumer group for Logic Apps
Use Azure Event Hubs trigger in Logic Apps workflow
Consider Capture feature for archiving

result.rationale: Event Grid handles push-based fan-out to many subscribers with filtering by event type. No polling — subscribers receive events as HTTP webhooks.
nextSteps: Create Event Grid Topic (custom events) or use built-in system topics
Each subscriber Logic App registers as an HTTP webhook subscription
Filter by event type to route to the right subscriber

result.recommendation: Azure Service Bus Topics with Subscriptions
result.rationale: Service Bus topics are the direct replacement for BizTalk MessageBox pub/sub. SQL filters on subscriptions replace BizTalk promoted property filter expressions.

configNotes.sqlFilter: Replaces BizTalk send port filter expressions

nextSteps: Create Service Bus topic
Create one subscription per subscriber with SQL filter
Publisher Logic App sets userProperties to match filter values
Each subscriber Logic App triggers on its subscription

result.rationale: Service Bus is the cloud-native replacement for MSMQ. Supports all key MSMQ features: dead-lettering, message deferral, scheduled messages, TTL.

configNotes.transactionalMSMQ: Use Service Bus sessions for transactional ordering
configNotes.deadLetterQueue: Built-in DLQ replaces MSMQ journal queue

nextSteps: Create Service Bus namespace and queue
Enable sessions if transactional ordering is required
Update MSMQ producers to use Service Bus SDK
Logic Apps trigger on Service Bus queue

result.rationale: Reliable queuing with built-in dead-lettering, retry, TTL. Preferred for async point-to-point messaging.
nextSteps: Create Service Bus namespace and queue
Use peekLock receive mode in Logic Apps for reliable processing
Complete message on success, dead-letter on failure

errorHandlingPattern.description: Determine the correct error handling pattern for a migration scenario

check_needs_retry.question: Should the action be retried on transient failure?

check_needs_dlq.question: Should failed messages be preserved for later reprocessing (dead-letter queue)?

check_needs_compensation.question: Does failure require undoing previously completed steps (compensation)?

check_needs_notification.question: Should someone be notified on failure?

result.rationale: Action-level retry policy handles transient failures without workflow redesign.
nextSteps: Add retryPolicy block to the action definition
Use exponential for downstream service protection
Set count to match BizTalk adapter retry setting
After retries exhausted → combine with DLQ or terminate

result.recommendation: Service Bus Dead Letter Queue pattern
result.rationale: Failed messages preserved in DLQ for analysis and reprocessing. Matches BizTalk suspended queue semantics.
nextSteps: Use peekLock receive mode (not receiveAndDelete)
Wrap processing in Scope action
On Scope failure (runAfter: FAILED): call deadLetterMessage action
On Scope success: call completeMessage action
Build separate DLQ processing workflow for resubmit

result.recommendation: Explicit compensation actions in Scope catch branch
result.rationale: No built-in Compensate shape in Logic Apps. Design undo actions explicitly in the Scope's catch branch.
warnings: Each compensating action must be idempotent
Partial compensation is possible — track which steps completed using variables
Document the compensation strategy clearly for operations team
nextSteps: Wrap forward actions in Scope
Add catch actions with runAfter: {ScopeName: [FAILED]}
Each catch action undoes one forward action (delete record, publish reversal, etc.)
Use result() to get details of which step failed
Wrap workflow in Scope action
Add notification action (email/Teams/Service Bus) with runAfter: {ScopeName: [FAILED]}
Include error details: result('ScopeName')[0]['error']['message']
Include workflow run link for investigation
Add Terminate action with runStatus=Failed
Set runError.message to describe the failure
Monitor via Azure Monitor alerts on failed runs
